notes: Can overwhelm smaller plants; spreads by rhizomes

facts.title: Beneficial Insects
facts.sub: Attracts predatory insects that eat garden pests

fun_facts.text: Attracts predatory insects like lacewings and hoverflies that eat garden pests like aphids.
Spreads by underground rhizomes to form dense colonies — nature's own weed suppressor.
The name "Anemone" comes from Greek anemos (wind) — these are the windflowers.

wildlife.title: Pollen Specialist
wildlife.desc: Visited by small native bees that specialize in collecting anemone pollen

wildlife.title: Beneficial Insects
wildlife.desc: Flowers attract predatory insects that provide natural pest control

planting_steps: Plant in moist soil with partial shade for best results
Space 12–18″ apart — it will fill in quickly via rhizomes
Use a root barrier if you need to contain its spread in small gardens

notes: Short-lived (3-5 years) but self-seeds readily

fun_facts.text: The red and yellow flowers are perfectly shaped for hummingbird bills — each spur holds a drop of nectar.
Self-seeds freely in the garden, and seedlings may produce new color variations.
Native Americans used tiny amounts of crushed seeds as a love charm and perfume.

wildlife.title: Hummingbird Favorite
wildlife.desc: One of the first nectar sources for Ruby-throated Hummingbirds in spring

wildlife.title: Long-Tongued Bees
wildlife.desc: Bumblebees and mason bees visit for nectar and pollen

wildlife.title: Butterfly Nectar
wildlife.desc: Swallowtails and skippers feed from the tubular flowers

planting_steps: Plant in part shade with well-drained soil — it likes woodland edges
Scatter seeds in fall for spring germination, or transplant in early spring
Let spent flowers go to seed for natural self-sowing each year

notes: Pollinated by flies; host plant for Pipevine Swallowtail (Battus philenor)

fun_facts.text: The only larval host for the Pipevine Swallowtail butterfly in our region.
Crush a leaf or root to release a spicy ginger scent — though it's not related to culinary ginger.
Flowers bloom at ground level, hidden under the leaves, and are pollinated by ground-crawling beetles.

wildlife.title: Pipevine Swallowtail Host
wildlife.desc: Critical larval food source for this declining butterfly species

wildlife.title: Beetle Pollinated
wildlife.desc: Ground-level flowers attract small beetles and flies as pollinators

planting_steps: Plant in full to part shade in rich, moist soil
Space rhizomes 12″ apart — they'll slowly spread to form a lush carpet
Mulch with leaf litter to mimic its natural woodland habitat

notes: First year forms rosette, flowers second year then dies. Self-seeds.

fun_facts.text: Despite its name, this is actually a biennial — it grows leaves the first year and flowers the second.
The star-shaped blue flowers only last one day each, but new ones open continuously for weeks.

wildlife.title: Native Bee Favorite
wildlife.desc: Especially attractive to small sweat bees and leaf-cutter bees

wildlife.title: Butterfly Nectar
wildlife.desc: Visited by various butterfly species for its abundant nectar

planting_steps: Sow seeds directly in moist, partly shaded soil in fall
Thin seedlings to 12″ apart in spring
Let plants self-seed freely to maintain a continuous colony year after year

notes: Host plant for Baltimore Checkerspot butterfly

fun_facts.text: The only larval host plant for the Baltimore Checkerspot butterfly, a species of conservation concern.
Named because the flower looks exactly like a turtle's head with its mouth open.
Only strong bumblebees can force open the closed flowers to reach the nectar inside.

wildlife.title: Baltimore Checkerspot Host
wildlife.desc: Essential breeding habitat for this rare and beautiful butterfly

wildlife.title: Bumblebee Specialist
wildlife.desc: Flowers require the strength of bumblebees to pry open for pollination

planting_steps: Plant in consistently moist to wet soil — perfect near streams or rain gardens
Part shade to full shade works best; it tolerates sun only if soil stays moist
Pinch stems in early June to encourage bushier growth and more blooms

notes: Dramatic tall white flower spikes; formerly Cimicifuga racemosa

fun_facts.text: Dramatic flower spires reach 3–5 feet tall, earning it the nickname "fairy candles."
Used for centuries by Native Americans and now widely studied for menopausal symptom relief.
The flowers have a musky scent that attracts specialized flies and beetles as pollinators.

wildlife.title: Fly & Beetle Pollinated
wildlife.desc: Unusual musky fragrance attracts specialized fly and beetle pollinators

wildlife.title: Bumble Bee Resource
wildlife.desc: Bumblebees also visit for pollen, especially in shadier sites

planting_steps: Plant in rich, moist soil in part to full shade — woodland edges are ideal
Space 2–3 feet apart; it forms impressive clumps over time
Mulch generously with composted leaves to keep roots cool and moist

notes: Pollinated exclusively by bumblebees which can pry open the closed flowers

facts.title: Bumblebee Exclusive
facts.sub: Only bumblebees can pry open the closed flowers

fun_facts.text: The flowers never fully open — only bumblebees are strong enough to pry them apart for nectar.
One of the most intensely blue wildflowers in North America, blooming when little else does in late fall.
Named "gentian" after King Gentius of ancient Illyria, who reputedly discovered the plant's medicinal properties.

wildlife.title: Bumblebee Exclusive
wildlife.desc: Only bumblebees have the strength and technique to force open the closed petals

wildlife.title: Late Season Nectar
wildlife.desc: Provides critical fuel for migrating butterflies in autumn

planting_steps: Plant in moist, rich soil in partial shade — meadow edges work well
Do not let soil dry out during the first growing season
Patience is key — plants are slow to establish but long-lived once settled

notes: Pollinated exclusively by bumblebees; feathery seed heads are ornamental

facts.title: Ornamental Seeds
facts.sub: Feathery pink seed heads extend the show

fun_facts.text: Named for its feathery pink seed heads that look like wisps of smoke drifting across the prairie.
One of the earliest spring bloomers — its nodding pink flowers appear before most other wildflowers.
The fernlike foliage turns brilliant red and orange in fall, providing a second season of interest.

wildlife.title: Early Bee Food
wildlife.desc: One of the first nectar sources for emerging queen bumblebees in spring

wildlife.title: Butterfly Nectar
wildlife.desc: Early spring butterflies visit the nodding pink flowers

planting_steps: Plant in full sun with dry, well-drained or rocky soil
Seeds need cold stratification — sow outdoors in fall for best results
Space 8–12″ apart in rock gardens or dry meadow areas

notes: Spreads by rhizomes to form colonies

facts.title: Bird Food
facts.sub: Seeds feed goldfinches & other birds in autumn

fun_facts.text: Seeds feed goldfinches, chickadees, and other songbirds throughout autumn and winter.
Unlike most sunflowers, this species thrives in partial shade at woodland edges.
Spreads by underground rhizomes to form cheerful golden colonies in late summer.

wildlife.title: Bird Food
wildlife.desc: Seeds are a key fall food source for goldfinches, sparrows, and juncos

wildlife.title: Pollinator Hub
wildlife.desc: Attracts bees, butterflies, and beneficial wasps to its broad flower heads

planting_steps: Plant in partial shade to full sun with average to moist soil
Space 18–24″ apart — rhizomes will fill in gaps over time
Cut back by half in early June if you want shorter, bushier plants

notes: Primary pollinator is ruby-throated hummingbird

facts.title: Short-Lived Perennial
facts.sub: Parent plant fades but readily self-seeds

fun_facts.text: The brilliant red color is perfectly tuned to attract hummingbirds, which are its primary pollinator.
A short-lived perennial — the parent plant fades after 2–3 years but readily self-seeds nearby.
Named after the red robes worn by Roman Catholic cardinals.

wildlife.title: Hummingbird Magnet
wildlife.desc: Primary pollinator is the Ruby-throated Hummingbird, drawn to its vivid red

wildlife.title: Swallowtail Nectar
wildlife.desc: Swallowtail butterflies also visit the tubular flowers for nectar

planting_steps: Plant in moist to wet soil along stream banks or in rain gardens
Part shade is ideal; full sun works only if soil stays consistently moist
Allow plants to self-seed by leaving spent stalks standing through winter

notes: Host plant for endangered Karner Blue butterfly; nitrogen fixer

facts.title: Endangered Species Host
facts.sub: Only host for the rare Karner Blue butterfly

fun_facts.text: The only host plant for the federally endangered Karner Blue butterfly.
Like other legumes, it fixes nitrogen in the soil, enriching the ground for neighboring plants.
Historically dependent on periodic fire to keep competing vegetation at bay.

wildlife.title: Karner Blue Host
wildlife.desc: Critical and sole food source for the rare Karner Blue butterfly larvae

wildlife.title: Bumble Bee Visited
wildlife.desc: Bumblebees work the pea-like flowers, tripping the pollination mechanism

planting_steps: Plant in full sun with sandy, well-drained soil — it hates wet feet
Nick or scarify seeds before planting to improve germination
Do not fertilize — it makes its own nitrogen and prefers lean soil

notes: Profuse self-seeder; short-lived biennial/perennial

fun_facts.text: Unlike its cousin Black-Eyed Susan, this species has a dark brown-purple center cone.
One of the toughest native wildflowers — thrives in poor soils where others struggle.

wildlife.title: Bee & Butterfly Buffet
wildlife.desc: Flat flower heads provide easy landing pads for all pollinators

wildlife.title: Winter Bird Food
wildlife.desc: Seed heads feed goldfinches and sparrows through the cold months

planting_steps: Plant in full sun with any well-drained soil — even poor, dry clay
Direct sow seeds in fall for natural cold stratification
Leave seed heads standing all winter for birds and self-seeding

notes: Red berries in fall attract birds (ruffed grouse)

facts.title: Bird Food
facts.sub: Red berries attract birds in fall

fun_facts.text: Produces clusters of bright red berries in fall that attract thrushes, catbirds, and other songbirds.
Distinguished from true Solomon's Seal by its fluffy white flower clusters at the stem tip.
The arching stems and graceful form make it an elegant shade garden focal point.

wildlife.title: Bird Berry Bush
wildlife.desc: Red fall berries are eagerly eaten by thrushes, catbirds, and grouse

wildlife.title: Pollinator Friendly
wildlife.desc: Tiny fragrant flowers attract small bees and flower flies

planting_steps: Plant in rich, moist woodland soil in part to full shade
Space 18″ apart; rhizomes slowly form graceful colonies
Top-dress with composted leaves annually to maintain rich soil

notes: Host plant for Buckeye butterfly larvae; elegant spire-like flower stalks

fun_facts.text: Larval host for the Common Buckeye butterfly, which feeds on its leaves.
Elegant white flower spires can reach 4–6 feet, making it a dramatic back-of-border plant.
Named after Dr. Culver, a colonial physician who used the root as a powerful medicinal purgative.

wildlife.title: Buckeye Host Plant
wildlife.desc: Caterpillars of the Common Buckeye butterfly feed on the foliage

wildlife.title: Pollinator Beacon
wildlife.desc: White spires attract a wide variety of bees, wasps, and butterflies

planting_steps: Plant in full sun to part shade in moist, rich soil
Space 2–3 feet apart; it gets large and impressive
Stake in windy locations or plant behind shorter perennials for support

notes: Stunning copper-orange fall/winter color; host plant for skipper butterflies

fun_facts.text: Fall foliage turns stunning copper-orange to burgundy, with fluffy white seed heads that glow in winter light.
Once the dominant grass of the tallgrass prairie, supporting vast ecosystems of wildlife.
Host plant for several skipper butterflies, including the Dusted Skipper and Cobweb Skipper.

wildlife.title: Skipper Host
wildlife.desc: Larval food plant for multiple skipper butterfly species

wildlife.title: Bird Nesting Cover
wildlife.desc: Dense clumps provide sheltered nest sites for ground-nesting birds

wildlife.title: Winter Shelter
wildlife.desc: Standing dried stems provide crucial winter cover for small mammals

planting_steps: Plant in full sun with dry to average, well-drained soil
Space 18–24″ apart in groups for a natural prairie look
Cut back to 4–6″ in late winter before new growth emerges

notes: Wind-pollinated; extremely adaptable native sedge

fun_facts.text: One of the best native lawn alternatives for shady areas — stays green and needs no mowing.
Forms soft, flowing mounds of evergreen foliage that look good year-round.

wildlife.title: Butterfly Host
wildlife.desc: Larval food for several sedge-feeding skipper butterflies

wildlife.title: Ground Cover Habitat
wildlife.desc: Dense low growth provides shelter for ground-nesting birds and toads

planting_steps: Plant in part to full shade in moist, well-drained soil
Space 8–12″ apart for a flowing groundcover effect
No mowing needed — just comb out dead foliage in early spring

notes: Nitrogen fixer; deep taproot makes it hard to transplant

fun_facts.text: During the American Revolution, colonists brewed its dried leaves as a caffeine-free tea substitute when boycotting British tea.
This plant thrives after fire. Its deep taproot can resprout from 6 feet underground.
Native Americans used the root bark to make a red dye and the leaves as a remedy for respiratory ailments.

wildlife.title: Bee Magnet
wildlife.desc: Supports 40+ species of native bees, including bumblebees and mining bees

wildlife.title: Butterfly Host Plant
wildlife.desc: Larval host for Spring Azure and Mottled Duskywing butterflies

wildlife.title: Bird Habitat
wildlife.desc: Dense branching offers nesting cover; seeds feed songbirds in fall

wildlife.title: Soil Life
wildlife.desc: Roots partner with Frankia bacteria, creating tiny nitrogen factories underground

planting_steps: Choose a spot with good drainage — this plant hates wet feet
Dig a hole twice the width of the root ball, same depth as the pot
Backfill with native soil (no amendments needed), water deeply, and mulch 2–3″ around the base

seasonal_tips.0: January: Plan your spring garden layout — New Jersey Tea pairs beautifully with Little Bluestem and Black-Eyed Susan.
seasonal_tips.1: February: Still dormant — perfect time to pick your planting spot. Remember: good drainage is key!
seasonal_tips.2: March: Watch for new growth. Prune dead branches before leaves emerge, but go easy — it blooms on old wood.
seasonal_tips.3: April: Prime planting time! Water deeply after planting and give it space to spread.
seasonal_tips.4: May: New growth is underway. Water weekly until established, then you can mostly leave it alone.
seasonal_tips.5: June: Buds are forming. Back off the watering — this plant actually prefers it on the dry side.
seasonal_tips.6: July: Bloom time! Watch for the parade of pollinators visiting those fluffy white flower clusters.
seasonal_tips.7: August: Still blooming. Leave spent flowers — they'll become seeds that songbirds love in fall.
seasonal_tips.8: September: Seeds are ripening. Cardinals and sparrows will thank you for leaving them on the plant.
seasonal_tips.9: October: Leaves turning golden yellow. Leave everything standing for winter interest and wildlife shelter.
seasonal_tips.10: November: Going dormant. A thin layer of leaf mulch around the base is all it needs for winter.
seasonal_tips.11: December: Resting underground. A great time to plan which native companions to add next spring!

notes: One of few plants blooming in late fall; pollinated by owlet moths active in cold weather

facts.title: Moth-Pollinated
facts.sub: Owlet moths pollinate in cold late-fall weather

fun_facts.text: Blooms in late October–November when almost nothing else is flowering — a true garden surprise.
Pollinated by owlet moths that fly in cold late-fall weather when other insects are dormant.
Seed capsules explode when ripe, shooting seeds up to 30 feet from the parent plant!

wildlife.title: Moth Pollinated
wildlife.desc: Owlet moths brave the cold to pollinate these late-season flowers

wildlife.title: Bird Shelter
wildlife.desc: Large spreading form provides excellent year-round bird habitat

planting_steps: Plant in part shade to shade in moist, acidic to neutral soil
Give it room — mature specimens spread 15–20 feet wide
Prune only right after flowering if needed; blooms form on old wood

notes: NOT invasive like Japanese honeysuckle; excellent hummingbird plant

fun_facts.text: A non-invasive native alternative to the destructive Japanese honeysuckle.
Tubular red flowers are perfectly designed for hummingbird bills — a top hummingbird plant.
Produces clusters of bright red berries in fall that feed migrating songbirds.

wildlife.title: Hummingbird Magnet
wildlife.desc: One of the most reliable plants for attracting Ruby-throated Hummingbirds

wildlife.title: Clearwing Moth Host
wildlife.desc: Larval food for the Snowberry Clearwing and Hummingbird Clearwing moths

wildlife.title: Berry Feast
wildlife.desc: Red berries are devoured by thrushes, waxwings, and other songbirds in fall

planting_steps: Plant near a fence, trellis, or arbor — this vine needs something to climb
Full sun to part shade; more sun means more flowers
Water regularly the first year, then it's quite drought tolerant once established

notes: Host plant for Clematis Clearwing moth; fluffy seed heads in fall

facts.title: Moth Host
facts.sub: Larval food for Clematis Clearwing moth

fun_facts.text: Larval food for the Clematis Clearwing moth, a beautiful day-flying moth.
The fluffy, silvery seed heads in fall are as ornamental as the flowers — great for winter arrangements.
This is the native clematis — a vigorous vine that covers fences and dead trees with white flowers.

wildlife.title: Moth Host
wildlife.desc: Larval food for the Clematis Clearwing moth and Virgin Tiger moth

wildlife.title: Pollinator Haven
wildlife.desc: Masses of small white flowers attract dozens of bee and butterfly species

planting_steps: Plant at the base of a fence, trellis, or shrub it can climb
Full sun to part shade in moist, well-drained soil
Prune hard in late winter if it gets too vigorous

notes: Late-season bloomer, extremely important for fall pollinators; aromatic foliage

fun_facts.text: Crush a leaf to enjoy the pleasant spicy scent — rare among asters.
One of the last flowers blooming in fall, providing crucial late-season nectar for migrating monarchs.

wildlife.title: Monarch Fuel Stop
wildlife.desc: Critical late-season nectar for migrating Monarch butterflies

wildlife.title: Native Bee Favorite
wildlife.desc: Attracts a wide diversity of native bees in fall when little else blooms

planting_steps: Plant in full sun with dry to average, well-drained soil
Space 18–24″ apart; it mounds to about 3 feet wide
Cut back by half in early June for a more compact shape and more blooms

notes: Tolerates dry shade once established; excellent woodland ground cover

fun_facts.text: Flowers range from pale pink to deep lavender — every plant is slightly different.
Seed pods have a spring-loaded mechanism that catapults seeds several feet from the parent plant.

wildlife.title: Spring Bee Resource
wildlife.desc: Important early nectar and pollen source for emerging native bees

wildlife.title: Butterfly Visited
wildlife.desc: Swallowtails and blues visit the open-faced flowers in spring

Space 12–18″ apart for a natural woodland carpet
Let seed pods mature and fling seeds for natural spreading

notes: Deep-throated flowers attract long-tongued bees and butterflies

fun_facts.text: A butterfly magnet — swallowtails hover around the fragrant flower clusters all day.
The ancestor of many popular garden phlox cultivars, but this wild form is more disease-resistant.

wildlife.title: Swallowtail Favorite
wildlife.desc: Long-tongued butterflies especially love the tubular fragrant flowers

wildlife.title: Bee Nectar Source
wildlife.desc: Bumblebees and long-tongued native bees visit for nectar

Space 12–18″ apart; good air circulation prevents powdery mildew
Deadhead spent flowers to encourage reblooming

notes: Spreads by stolons to form attractive colonies; semi-evergreen foliage

Named for the frothy, foam-like appearance of its white flower clusters in spring.
Maple-shaped leaves often develop burgundy markings along the veins in fall.

wildlife.title: Native Bee Visited
wildlife.desc: Small native bees and sweat bees visit the delicate spring flowers

planting_steps: Plant in part to full shade in moist, rich, acidic soil
Space 12″ apart; stolons spread to form a lush carpet
Mulch with shredded leaves to mimic the forest floor

notes: Flowers open sequentially from bottom to top; tolerates wet soil

fun_facts.text: A pollinator powerhouse — attracts dozens of bee and butterfly species to its purple flower spikes.
Used in traditional herbalism for calming and digestive remedies for centuries.

wildlife.title: Bee & Wasp Magnet
wildlife.desc: Flower spikes are covered with native bees, wasps, and flies all summer

wildlife.title: Butterfly Nectar
wildlife.desc: Skippers, hairstreaks, and small butterflies visit the tiny flowers

wildlife.title: Seed Food
wildlife.desc: Small birds feed on the abundant seeds in late summer

planting_steps: Plant in full sun to part shade in moist to wet soil
Perfect for rain gardens, pond edges, and low wet spots
Self-seeds readily — remove spent stalks if you want to limit spread

notes: Elegant fan-shaped fronds on dark wiry stems; no flowers (fern reproduces by spores)

fun_facts.text: The delicate, fan-shaped fronds on jet-black stems make this one of the most elegant native ferns.
The name "maidenhair" refers to the fine, hair-like black stems (stipes) of the fronds.

wildlife.desc: Dense fronds create cool, moist microclimates for salamanders and frogs

Keep soil consistently moist — this fern will not tolerate drought
Shelter from wind, which can damage the delicate fronds

notes: Rose hips provide winter food for birds; host plant for several moth species

facts.title: Bird Food
facts.sub: Rose hips feed birds through winter

fun_facts.text: Rose hips persist through winter, feeding birds when other food is scarce.
Unlike invasive multiflora rose, this native rose is well-behaved and only mildly thorny.
Rose hips are rich in vitamin C — historically used to make teas and jams.

wildlife.title: Native Bee Haven
wildlife.desc: Simple open flowers provide easy access to pollen for native bees

wildlife.title: Winter Bird Food
wildlife.desc: Rose hips feed cedar waxwings, robins, and mockingbirds all winter

wildlife.title: Butterfly Nectar
wildlife.desc: Fragrant pink flowers attract a variety of butterflies

Give it 3–4 feet of space; it forms a low, arching shrub
Prune out dead canes in late winter; leave rose hips for birds

Historically associated with St. John's Day (June 24) when it typically begins blooming.

wildlife.title: Pollen Source
wildlife.desc: Abundant stamens produce copious pollen for native bees and flower beetles

wildlife.title: Butterfly Visited
wildlife.desc: Various butterflies visit the large, open golden flowers

wildlife.desc: Fragrant white flowers attract bumblebees in early summer

Do not disturb once established — it spreads slowly on its own

wildlife.title: Swallowtail Host

wildlife.desc: Long-tongued butterflies like swallowtails favor the tubular flowers

wildlife.title: Spring Bee Resource

fun_facts.text: Named "ironweed" for its incredibly tough stems that resist breaking even in strong winds.

wildlife.title: Monarch Fuel
wildlife.desc: Rich nectar source for migrating Monarch butterflies in late summer

wildlife.title: Bee Magnet
wildlife.desc: Bumblebees swarm the vivid purple flower clusters

Space 2–3 feet apart; it gets very tall

facts.title: Well-Behaved
facts.sub: Does not spread aggressively like other violets

wildlife.title: Amphibian Habitat
wildlife.desc: Dense fronds create cool, moist shelter for frogs, toads, and salamanders

wildlife.title: Nesting Cover
wildlife.desc: Low fern thickets provide hiding spots for ground-nesting birds

planting_steps: Plant in part to full shade in rich, moist, acidic soil

planting_steps: Plant in part to full shade in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil
